dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 8435 IH: War Powers Priority Procedures Modernization Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mr. Barrett (for himself and Mr. Golden of Maine) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned

form.official-title: To amend the War Powers Resolution to provide for priority procedures for joint resolutions, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the War Powers Priority Procedures Modernization Act.

subsection.text: Section 5(c) of the War Powers Resolution (50 U.S.C. 1544) is amended by inserting or joint resolution after concurrent resolution.

subsection.header: Congressional priority procedures for concurrent resolution or joint resolution
subsection.text: Section 7 of the War Powers Resolution (50 U.S.C. 1546) is amended—

paragraph.text: in the section heading, by inserting or joint resolution after concurrent resolution;
by inserting or joint resolution after concurrent resolution each place it appears; and
by adding at the end the following:

subsection.text: In the case that a joint resolution passed by both Houses should be vetoed by the President, the time for debate in consideration of the veto message on such joint resolution shall be limited to 20 hours in the Senate and 20 hours in the House of Representatives.

Notwithstanding section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985 (50 U.S.C. 1546a), the priority procedures under this section shall apply where a joint resolution is introduced in either House which requires the removal of United States Armed Forces engaged in hostilities outside the territory of the United States, its possessions and territories without a declaration of war or specific statutory authorization.
